coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of mesoporous recycled poly(ethylene terephthalate)/MWNT/carbon quantum dot nanocomposite from sustainable materials using ultrasonic waves: Application for methylene blue removal
coredata.dc:creator: Mallakpour, Shadpour
Behranvand, Vajiheh
coredata.dc:description: This study presents fabrication of recycled poly(ethylene terephthalate) (rPET) reinforced with decorated multiwalled carbon nanotubes by carbon quantum dots (MWNT/CQD)s. The resulting MWNT/CQDs and rPET/MWNT/CQD composites were characterized by various analyses. The sorption performances of the manufactured rPET/MWNT/CQD composite were assessed for the methylene blue dye removal. The obtained composite adsorbed 98% dye from water. The study of isotherm and kinetic models showed that Langmuir and Dubinin–Radushkevich and pseudo-second-order kinetic models gave the better fitting. Extracted parameters form isotherm models exhibited favorable adsorption on rPET/MWNT/CQD composite. Furthermore, the reusability tests indicated that the composite can be regenerated and reused for at least four cycles with high removal efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Multiwalled carbon nanotubes
Recycling
Dye removal

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of conjugated ox-MWNTs with CQDs (MWNT/CQD)s
Thermal decomposition behaviors of the hybrids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the fluorescence CQDs preparation in one step under hydrothermal process.
Preparation steps of MWNT/CQDs.
FT-IR spectra of (a) pure CQD, (b) ox-MWNT, (c) MWNT/CQD, (d) pure rPET, and (e) rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%.
Raman spectra of (a) ox-MWNT and (b) MWNT/CQD.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of pure CQDs dispersed in deionized water. (b) PL spectra of pure CQDs, dispersed in deionized water, upon various excitation wavelengths. (c) PL spectra of pure CQDs and MWNT/CQDs upon 400 nm excitation wavelength.
XRD patterns of (a) ox-MWNT and (b) MWNT/CQD.
FE-SEM micrographs of carb-MWNT (a, b) at two different magnifications.
FE-SEM micrographs of ox-MWNT (a–c) and MWNT/CQD (d–f) at three different magnifications.
FE-SEM micrographs of (a) pure rPET, (b) rPET/MWNT/CQD 1 wt%, (c) rPET/MWNT/CQD 2 wt%, and (d) rPET/MWNT/CQD 4 wt%.
EDX spectra of (a) ox-MWNT and (b) MWNT/CQD.
TEM micrographs of (a) carb-MWNT, (b) pure CQD (Adopted from Yin et al., 2013, with kind permission of RSC), (c–e) MWNT/CQD at different magnifications.
(a, b) Two-dimensional and (c) three-dimensional AFM images of MWNT/CQDs and (d) related profiles.
TEM micrographs of rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% at various magnifications.
(A) TGA thermograms of (a) carb-MWNT, (b) ox-MWNT and (c) MWNT/CQD, (B) DTG thermograms of (a) carb-MWNT, (b) ox-MWNT, and (c) MWNT/CQD.
The weight loss (TG) and its derivative (DTG) thermograms as a function temperature for (a) pure rPET, (b) rPET/MWNT/CQD 1 wt%, (c) rPET/MWNT/CQD 2 wt%, and rPET/MWNT/CQD 4 wt%.
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of (a) ox-MWNT and (a′) MWNT/CQD, and pore size distribution curves of (b) ox-MWNT and (b′) MWNT/CQD.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms (inset) the related pore size distribution curves for the (a) pure rPET, and (b) rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%, (c) comparison between adsorption capacity of different samples (C0: 20 mg L−1, contact time: 48 h, pH 8), and (d) digital images of pure rPET, and rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% before and after methylene blue (MB) adsorption.
Effect of (a) pH, (b) determining pHPZC (c) effect of dosage and, (d) contact time on sorption behavior of MB (C0 = 10 mg L−1, V = 10 mL, temperature at 298 K).

Plots of (a) pseudo–first–order kinetics, (b) pseudo–second–order kinetics, and (c) intra particle diffusion models of MB onto the rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% (C0 = 10 mg L−1, Sorbent dosage: 60 mg, pH = 8, T = 298 K).
(A) The Langmuir, (B) Freundlich and (C) Dubinin−Radushkevich isotherms for the MB on the rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% surface (Sorbent dosage: 60 mg, pH = 8, T = 298 K).
Possible interactions between MB and rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%.
Reuse cycles for rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% (C0 = 10 ppm, pH = 8).
The characteristic temperatures of the decomposition of the pure rPET and rPET/MWNT/CQD composites in argon.
BET analysis outcomes for the samples.
Parameters of various models for the MB adsorption onto rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%.
The parameters for Langmuir, Freundlich and DR isotherms for MB sorption onto the rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%.
Adsorption capacities of different sorbents for MB removal.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of mesoporous recycled poly(ethylene terephthalate)/MWNT/carbon quantum dot nanocomposite from sustainable materials using ultrasonic waves: Application for methylene blue removal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Organic Polymer Chemistry Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, Islamic Republic of Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156–83111, Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Organic Polymer Chemistry Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, Islamic Republic of Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents fabrication of recycled poly(ethylene terephthalate) (rPET) reinforced with decorated multiwalled carbon nanotubes by carbon quantum dots (MWNT/CQD)s. The resulting MWNT/CQDs and rPET/MWNT/CQD composites were characterized by various analyses. The sorption performances of the manufactured rPET/MWNT/CQD composite were assessed for the methylene blue dye removal. The obtained composite adsorbed 98% dye from water. The study of isotherm and kinetic models showed that Langmuir and Dubinin–Radushkevich and pseudo-second-order kinetic models gave the better fitting. Extracted parameters form isotherm models exhibited favorable adsorption on rPET/MWNT/CQD composite. Furthermore, the reusability tests indicated that the composite can be regenerated and reused for at least four cycles with high removal efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: A novel nanostructured carbon group (< 10 nm) is carbon-based quantum dots (QD)s including of carbon QDs (C-dots or CQDs) and graphene QDs (QGDs) (Wang and Hu, 2014). CQDs as a zero-dimensional carbon with sp2 carbon construction are an interesting carbon nanoparticles group, which have exceptional benefits such as low cost and toxicity, ease of preparation as well as functionalization and biocompatibility. Thus, they were employed in many applications and can be replaced by conventional semiconductor QDs contain noxious and/or costly heavy metals (Bai et al., 2016; Lim et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015). Besides, there is a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups such as CO, OH, COOH and NH2 on the surface of CQDs that causes high water solubility and polymerization capability with different substances (Zuo et al., 2015). According to mentioned unique properties of CQDs, they can be used as a promising material for elimination of a variety of inorganic and organic pollutants. But as it was stated they have high-solubility in water (Zhang et al., 2014). One of the solutions to prevent this problem is creation of a robust and stable covalent bonds between a carbon nanotubes (CNT)s as supports and CQDs and manufacture of CNT/CQD hybrids (Skaltsas et al., 2016). Skaltsas et al. attached CQDs onto oxidized multiwalled carbon nanotubes and prepared MWNT/CQDs and introduced them as ideal candidates in energy conversion uses (Skaltsas et al., 2016). Chen et al. fabricated MWNTs/CQDs and proposed them for photovoltaic applications (Chen et al., 2014). MWNT/CQDs were used by Bai et al. as electrochemical sensor to detect H2O2 in living cells (Bai et al., 2016). In 2017, Mandani et al. prepared the CNT/CQDs and obtained remarkable decrease in their cytotoxicity in comparison to unfunctionalized or bovine serum albumin coated CNTs (Mandani et al., 2017).

One of the most used polyester resin is poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) which have potential industrial applications in construction of fibers, bottles, films, and engineering plastics (Gorrasi et al., 2015; Maalouf et al., 2018). Due to high toughness, simple manufacturing, and low fabrication cost, using of this polymer has been increased which lead to environmental pollution accompanied by environmental risks. Presently, recycling of polymers not only has a contribution in environmental protection but also cause conservation of petroleum sources (Mallakpour and Behranvand, 2017a, 2017b; 2017c; Shadpour Mallakpour and Behranvand, 2016a, b).

CNTs as one of the significant carbon allotropes have brilliant sorption capacity for organic and inorganic pollutant materials due to promising properties like as hollow and mesoporous structures and high surface area with large available external functionalized surfaces (Abbasi, 2017; Mallakpour and Behranvand, 2017d; Mallakpour and Khadem, 2016; Mallakpour and Soltanian, 2016). Conjugation of capping agents such as CQDs not only prevents the creation of CNT aggregates but also can enhance the elimination efficiency of the pollutants. However, employment of CNT/CQD hybrid as adsorbent for the pollution removal in the very fine powder forms not only needs hard solid/aqueous solution separation, which wastes the time but also the material costs is high. To increase interactions of sorbents with contaminants and make easy separation and regeneration as well as decrease the costs, production of advantageous organic-inorganic hybrid polymers have been encouraged (Samiey et al., 2014).

In this work, the obtained CQDs from green source were attached covalently onto the MWNTs through a simple method and were fully characterized. Then, different amounts of the attained hybrids (1, 2 and 4 wt%) were incorporated into the recycled PET matrix to obtain low-cost rPET/MWNT/CQD composites. Not only morphological and structural characteristics of the achieved composites were studied but also rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% was employed as a model for dye elimination from water. Effects of pH, adsorbent dosage, time, and concentration were investigated and adsorption isotherms determinations as well as kinetic studies were also performed. To the best of our knowledge there have been no reports on the incorporation of MWNT/CQD into the rPET matrix and employing it as adsorbent for dye removal.

The obtained red pepper from supermarket was washed for next usage. Carboxylated multiwalled CNTs (carb-MWNT)s (with inner and outer diameters of 5–10 nm and 10–20 nm, respectively, length ∼30 μm, purity > 95 wt% and carboxyl content 2.00 wt%) were provided by Neutrino Co. (Tehran, Iran). PET scraps were gained from chopping of post-consumer bottle wastes. Dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) was purchased from DAEJUNG, Korea. Methylene blue (MB), N,N
′ -carbonyl diimidazole (CDI), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAc) were supplied by Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany).

Raman measurements were done for suspension samples on a Takram P50C0R10 from Teksan (Turkey). The wavelength of excitation applied was 532 nm. The spectra were recorded in the range of 50–4000 cm−1 for all of the samples. Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained in KBr pellet by means of a Jasco-680 (Japan). UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were achieved for sample suspensions with a UV–Vis/NIR spectrometer (JASCO, V-570, Japan) in scanning range of 200–800 nm and RF-5301 PC, respectively. TESCAN MIRA II (Czechoslovakia) and HITACHI S-4160 (Japan) with acceleration voltages of 15 kV were applied for obtaining field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Philips CM120, Netherlands) was employed as well to observe the morphology details of the samples. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were recorded using a Bruker system (Germany) in contact mode. Theromogravimetric analysis (TGA) as well as differential thermogravimetic (DTG) of the samples were obtained with a PerkinElmer analyzer (STA 6000) (Waltham, USA) by heating samples from room temperature to 800 °C in an argon atmosphere. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were studied at 77 K on a BELSORP-miniII (Japan) instrument. Specific surface areas (SSA) and the pore-size distributions of the samples were determined due to the Brunauer, Emmett and Teller (BET) equation and the Barrett, Joyner, and Halenda (BJH) method, respectively. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a Philips X’PERT MPD (Germany). It has a copper target at the CuKα radiation with λ = 1.54 Å, a tube current of 35 mA and tube voltage of 40 kV. ZAG CHEMIE PTR 79 Co. (Tehran, Iran) was applied for the pH measurement.

CQDs were prepared according to work of Yin et al. (2013). Deionized (DI) water (1 mL) was added to fresh red pepper (4 g) and this mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave reactor and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. Then, the mixture was left for 24 h to cool down slowly. Next, to remove large black solids, the obtained mixture was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained light yellow supernatant having the CQDs was placed to ultra-filtration centrifuge tube (10000 MWCO) and centrifuged at 3000 rmp for 6 h. For further using, the attained solution was kept in a refrigerator.

To increase defects and amounts of functional groups content on the surface of nanotubes and to acquire oxidized MWNTs (ox-MWNT)s, carb-MWNTs (0.1 g) were dispersed in HNO3:H2SO4 (4 mL) (1:1) and refluxed at 140 °C for 2 h. After that, the mixture was kept overnight to CNTs settle down. Then, the supernatant was decanted and the black powders were centrifuged and washed several times with distilled water till pH reached to neutral state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of conjugated ox-MWNTs with CQDs (MWNT/CQD)s
ce:section.ce:para: ox-MWNTs (0.15 g) were distributed in DMF (16 mL) using ultrasonic probe (Topsonics ultrasonic liquid processors, Tehran, Iran, power: 100 W; work-frequency: 25000 Hz) for 10 min. Then, CDI (0.017 g) was added to this mixture and heated at 60–80 °C for 3 h while it was stirring. After this time, it was sonicated for 30 min. CQD (2 mL) was dispersed in 6 mL of DI water. Then, 5 mL of this solution was sonicated for 3 min and was added slowly to the CNT suspension and stirred overnight at room temperature. Because a very stable suspension was obtained which cannot be separated via centrifugation, first it was dried at 60 °C, and after that, the obtained powders were centrifuged and washed with distilled water several times and dried.

For the preparation of rPET/MWNT/CQD composites with different loadings of MWNT/CQDs (1, 2 and 4 wt%), first rPET (0.1 g) was dispersed in DMAc (10 mL) through ultrasonic irradiations for 7 min. Then, desired amounts of MWNT/CQD (0.001, 0.002 and 0.004 g) were added to this suspension and the obtained mixture was sonicated for 30 min. After this period of time, it was cast on Petri dish and dried at 100 °C. The attained composites are named rPET/MWNT/CQD 1 wt%, rPET/MWNT/CQD 2 wt% and rPET/MWNT/CQD 4 wt%.

To prepare dye solutions, MB was dissolved in distilled water. In each experiment, fresh dilutions were employed. Batch sorption experiments were done to optimize sorbent dosage, pH, time and concentration. All tests were directed in container containing 10 mL of MB solution and shaken at 170 rpm at room temperature for 24 h.

The influence of dosage was tested in the range of 20–70 mg for MB (contact time: 24 h at room temperature). To examine the pH effect, the range of 2–10 was selected (contact time: 24 h at room temperature) and solution pH was adjusted by adding HCl and NaOH (0.1 mol L−1). The contact time effect was determined from 5 to 2880 min (at optimum dosage and pH, and room temperature). Sorption isotherm tests were performed by changing initial MB concentrations (10–300 mg L−1) (at optimum dosage, pH, time and room temperature).

In the last part of the sorption in each part, the supernate was obtained using a micropipette and residual MB concentration was determined by UV–Vis spectroscopy at the maximum wavelength (665 nm). The sorption capacity (q e, mg g−1) and removal efficiency (R, %) were calculated using the following equations:
(1)
q e
=
(
C 0
−
C e
)
× V / m
(2)
R =
(
C 0
−
C e
)
× 100 /
C 0
where C 0, C e, V, and m are initial MB concentration (mg L−1), equilibrium MB concentration (mg L−1), volume of MB solution (L), and adsorbent weight (g), respectively.

The regeneration of the rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% loaded with MB pollutants, was accomplished by placing the sorbent into ethanol (30 mL) as the desorption agent under shaking at room temperature for 5 h. All cycles were accomplished in duplicate.

One of the promising routes for the production of CQDs which is green, inexpensive, and nontoxic is known as hydrothermal or solvothermal carbonization (Wang and Hu, 2014). Yin et al. not only applied from an environmentally friendly and low cost way, but also used from a red pepper as a green material. Thus, it was decided to use this benign and safe method for the preparation of CQDs (Fig. 1 ).

In this work, it was tried to conjunct CQDs on the surface of ox-MWNTs through esterification reaction by CDI as reaction activator with the help of ultrasonic irradiations. COOH groups on the surface of MWNTs reacted with CDI and changed to active ester groups (COOR). Esterification reaction happened between OH groups on the CQDs surfaces and carboxyl groups of the nanotubes (Fig. 2 ).

An outstanding approach to break up the agglomerates is ultrasonic technique. Ultrasound assisted method for the nanomaterial preparation proposes various benefits such as stability, unique particle size, morphology and crystalline nature compared to the traditional systems (Mallakpour et al., 2016; Mallakpour and Behranvand, 2016a, b; Shabana et al., 2016). Therefore, this technique was employed for the preparation of MWNT/CQD hybrids and also the manufacture of rPET/MWNT/CQD composites.

The FT-IR spectra of the samples are shown in Fig. 3 . Pure CQD showed characteristic peaks about 1628, 1425 and 3434 cm−1 [COO– symmetric stretching, in-plane bending/COO– asymmetric stretching (Pires et al., 2015) and stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups, respectively], 2851 and 2922 cm−1 (symmetric and asymmetric CH stretching) (Liqin et al., 2011). In the spectrum of MWNT/CQD, in addition to the peaks which were observed for pure CQD, peak at 1578 cm−1 [the stretching vibration of carbon skeleton of ox-MWNT (Mallakpour and Behranvand, 2017a)] and also a new peak at about 1724 cm−1 (CO in ester groups) were observed as well (Chen et al., 2014). FT-IR spectrum of pure PET after incorporation of MWNT/CQDs did not show any significant changes in vibration peaks due to low loading of filler except in OH stretching vibration which was broaden due to increasing of H-bonding after incorporation of MWNT/CQDs (Kotal et al., 2009).

Raman spectra of ox-MWNT and MWNT/CQD are shown in Fig. 4 . In the spectra, the presence of G-band at 1561 cm−1 shows the distinctive peak for all sp2 hybridized carbon allotropes. On the other hand, D-band which is primarily related to the disorder or defects in the sp2-hybridized carbon was observed at about 1340 cm−1. The ratio of band intensity ID/IG is proportionate with the number of defects on the MWNTs surface (Baro et al., 2013; Rebelo et al., 2016). According to Fig. 4, the ID/IG ratios for ox-MWNT, and MWNT/CQD were 0.94 and 0.85, respectively. As can be observed, the intensity of D-band decreased for the MWNT/CQD. This can be explained as the following reasons: (i) the reaction occurred between carboxyl groups on the nanotube walls and CQDs and direct modification of the MWNT walls didn't happen. (ii) or it could be concluded that the defect structures of MWNTs rather than their perfect sites mostly were decorated by CQDs, (iii) moreover, increase in IG intensity and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms for MWNT/CQD can be related to the presence of double bonds in CQDs (Hajova et al., 2015; Hussain and Islam, 2012; Kang et al., 2016). On the other hand, to estimate the purity and construction quality of MWNTs, the IG/ID ratio was studied (Yu et al., 2012). Their values for ox-MWNT and MWNT/CQD were 1.06 and 1.18, respectively. The higher IG/ID ratio of MWNT/CQD indicated a higher purity and structure quality of nanotubes after the QDs functionalization. As a result, it can be said that MWNTs functionalization by CQDs ‘mended’ the defects on the tubes surfaces.

UV–Vis spectra of ox-MWNT, CQD, and MWNT/CQD are indicated in Fig. 5 a. UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQD showed a broad peak in the range of 250–300 nm which is ascribed to the π conjugated aromatic system and the n–π* transition of the CO and other groups having oxygen (Wu et al., 2014). UV–Vis spectrum of MWNT/CQD showed that UV adsorption ability has been preserved after combination of CQDs with MWNTs. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the CQDs dispersed in DI water excited at diverse excitation wavelengths (300–400 nm) at room temperature are shown in Fig. 5b. Red shifts in the CQDs emission were observed when the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 400 nm and the highest intensity of the emission peak at 420 nm was achieved upon 400 nm excitation of CQDs. The PL features of MWNT/CQD were also studied and compared with those of pure CQDs (Fig. 5c). The distinctive emission of CQDs at 487 nm (upon excitation at 400 nm), showed red-shift about 10 nm and was quenched in MWNT/CQD. This phenomena was reported by Skaltsas et al., as well (Skaltsas et al., 2016). After irradiation of MWNT/CQD, an electron in CQDs is excited into higher state. The excited electron is quenched and transferred into the conduction bond of MWNTs. Consequently, light energy was stored as electrical energy in the obtained hybrid (Chen et al., 2014).

The XRD patterns of ox-MWNT and MWNT/CQD were depicted in Fig. 6 . In addition to diffraction peaks of nanotubes at 2θ of 26 and 43° related to the (002) and (100) planes of graphitic carbon, a low intensity broad heave placed at about 20° was observed too. Its interlayer spacing was calculated by Bragg's law and found to be 0.44 nm which was larger than that of graphite (0.34 nm). This is attributed to the presence of more functional groups on the CQD surface.

The crystallite size of carbon compounds can be studied by Lc. Lc is the thickness of crystallite (perpendicular to graphene sheets and along c-direction) which is measured from FWHM of peak detecting at ∼ 25° corresponding to (002) crystal planes (Feret, 1998; Sreeramoju, 2013). The crystallite size of ox-MWNT and MWNT/CQD can be determined by means of Scherrer equation by knowing the FWHM “(full width at half maximum)” of peaks of (002) crystal planes (Maldhure and Wankhade, 2017). The Scherrer equation is
(3)
L c
=K λ / βCosθ
where Lc is the crystallite size, K is the Scherrer or shape constant (∼0.9), λ is the radiation wavelength (1.542 Å), β is the FWHM of peak in radians and θ is one half of position of peak. The calculated value of Lc by the Scherrer equation for ox-MWNT was found to be about 2.04 nm, and its value for MWNT/CQD was 4.34 nm. As can be observed, the crystal thickness was increased by addition of CQDs representing an increase in interlayer spacing of graphitic planes.

Figs. 7–9
present the FE-SEM micrographs and EDX patterns in conjunction with related tables of carb-MWNTs, ox-MWNT and MWNT/CQD, respectively. The micrographs show no significant changes in morphology of carb-MWNTs after treating with HNO3:H2SO4 and it can be concluded that the morphology of carb-MWNTs with ox-MWNTs would be more or less similar (Fig. 7a and b and Fig. 8a–c). As can be observed clearly, the morphology of nanotubes has been changed after decoration by CQDs. It is worthy to point out that aggregations of MWNTs were decreased remarkably (Fig. 8d–f).

FE-SEM images of rPET and rPET/MWNT/CQD composites are represented in Fig. 9. Due to low contrast between MWNT/CQDs, rPET and the carbon film (substrate of used grid in FE-SEM), few nanotubes are observed in images but their presence and distribution state will be more obvious in further TEM micrographs.

To confirm CQDs conjugation on ox-MWNT, EDX analysis was recorded and studied (Fig. 10 ). The results showed that the oxygen percentage for ox-MWNT was 12 wt%. It can be said that this amount is ascribed to oxygen atoms of carboxylic groups on the surface of nanotubes. Oxygen content for MWNT/CQD increased significantly about 5 wt% compared to the ox-MWNT. This may be credited to the existence of CQDs with many oxygen-containing functional groups.

TEM micrographs of carb-MWNT, pure CQD (Yin et al., 2013) and MWNT/CQD are presented in Fig. 11 . Outer diameters of carb-MWNTs were about 10 nm. It was reported by Yin et al. (2013) that CQDs showed a separated morphology with mean diameters of ranging from 2 to 7 nm. Obviously, there is a fairly low contrast between CQDs and the carbon film (substrate of the grid which is used in TEM) so TEM imaging of these materials is not a forthright procedure. Furthermore, additional difficulties ascend throughout the MWNT/CQDs imaging. Clearly, as displayed in Fig. 11c–e, the tubes were surrounded by a thick layer which can be distinguished especially in Fig. 11e and d, as well. The presence of carbon thick layer can be confirmed by significant increasing of nanotubes diameters compared to the pristine ones from 10 nm to the range of 30–90 nm. There are black spherical dots on the surface of nanotubes which may be due to the presence of defects.

AFM analysis of MWNT/CQD was also recorded and shown in Fig. 12 . Fig. 12a and b shows two-dimensional and three-dimensional AFM images of MWNT/CQD. The presence of bright regions on the MWNTs surface (was shown with dash ellipses) can be related to the CQDs (Fig. 12a). The topography image obviously emphasized the well-connected MWNTs with the sphere-shaped CQDs. According to the line profiles extracted from AFM images, the MWNT average height was about 20 nm. The similar line on the parts with MWNT/CQDs conjugates was around 30 nm. This increasing which was about 10 nm can be related to the diameter of few CQDs. This showed successful conjugation of CQDs on the surface of nanotubes.

The distribution status of nanotubes in the rPET matrix was seen in TEM micrographs (Fig. 13 ). Homogeneous dispersion of MWNT/CQD in the rPET can be clearly observed in these micrographs. In other words, most of nanotubes were detached from each other without creating large bundles in the polymer matrix which shows good interfacial interaction between rPET and MWNT/CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal decomposition behaviors of the hybrids
ce:section.ce:para:
TGA–DTG thermograms for carb-MWNT, ox-MWNT and MWNT/CQD as well as TGA–DTG thermograms for pure rPET and rPET/MWNT/CQD composites are shown in Figs. 14 and 15
. Obviously, carb-MWNTs exhibited a small weight loss (6 wt%) from room temperature to 800 °C, due to the removal of the introduced oxygenated species, while this amount for ox-MWNTs was increased to 14 wt% after more acidification of MWNTs. Also, initial decomposition temperature of MWNTs was increased from 200 °C for carb-MWNT to 241 °C for ox-MWNTs. This may be due to the presence of more carboxyl groups on the ox-MWNT and creation of a hexagon hydrogen bond between the carboxyl group at the nanotubes surface which increased their activation energy and rose decomposition temperature, so, improved the thermal stability (Hsieh et al., 2010). Initial decomposition temperature of MWNTs for MWNT/CQDs reached to 256 °C that exhibited higher thermal stability compared to both carb-MWNTs and ox-MWNT due to the existence of more functional groups and increase in possibility of different interactions between them as well as increase in activation energy and decomposition temperature. A weight loss at about 360 °C which just can be observed in thermogram of MWNT/CQDs can be ascribed to the thermal degradation of CQDs. In addition, peaks at 670 and 790 °C in DTG thermogram of ox-MWNTs can be related to amorphous carbon weight loss and destruction of tubes due to high oxidation (Chen et al., 2004).

The temperature values of 2 and 10% weight loss (T 2%, T 10%) and the temperature at the maximum weight loss rate (T DTG) of pure rPET and rPET/MWNT/CQD composites are listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that the degradation happened in a single stage for pure rPET and rPET/MWNT/CQD composites. Onset degradation temperatures and values of residual weight at 800 °C showed low increase for composites compared to the pure rPET by the addition of MWNT/CQDs (Table 1).

The Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution plots (BJH-plots) corresponded to the ox-MWNT and MWNT/CQD can be observed in Fig. 16 and extracted data were listed in Table 2 . Due to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) system, the gained curves for both samples can be categorized as type-IV isotherms which indicate the presence of mesopores. Mean pore diameter (MPD) values were found in the mesopores ranges (2–50 nm) for both samples. It was apparent that the structural parameters of the MWNT/CQD were higher than those of the ox-MWNT. The SSA of MWNT/CQD was 143.18 m2 g−1, which was around two-times larger than ox-MWNT. This confirms the presence of CQDs on the surface of tubes. In addition, MPD increased after CQD functionalization which could be described by QD filling of small pores.

Fig. 17 indicates the Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution plots (BJH-plots) related to the pure rPET and rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% sorbents. The adsorption–desorption isotherms of both sorbents displayed hysteresis loops of type H3 at relative pressures (P/P0) from 0.8 to 1.0 representing the presence of mesoporous construction in the two sorbents. The BJH-plots also presented narrow unimodal pore size distribution centered at 7.98 nm (inset of Fig. 17). The total pore volume (TPV), MPD, and SSA of the mentioned sorbents were determined and listed in Table 2. The obtained TPV, MPD and SSA from the BET equation for rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% were 0.087 cm3 g−1, 49.20 nm, and 7.05 m2 g−1 which were much higher than pure rPET (0.045 cm3 g−1, 37.49 nm, and 4.85 m2 g−1, respectively). Obviously, the introduction of MWNT/CQDs caused such a noteworthy increase in the SSA and pore volumes. Increase in SSA can be due to the presence of MWNT/CQD which have large surface area. Actually, owing to the large surface areas, porous structures and π–π electrostatic interactions, the CNTs have been confirmed to possess huge potential as sorbents for eliminating organic pollutants. The increased MPD could be described by filling of small pores by filler loading.

The effect of MWNT/CQD loading percentages on MB removal was examined, and the results showed an increase in MB sorption capacity with increasing MWNT/CQD content (Fig. 17c). As can be seen, rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% showed the highest sorption capacity for MB elimination.

Also, the sorption capacity values of rPET/MWNT/CQD composites were compared with pure rPET, and rPET/carb-MWNT composite 4 wt% (without CQD) (Fig. 17c). The results were interesting and consistent with the BET outcomes. MB sorption capacity values for all rPET/MWNT/CQD composites were not only higher than rPET but also were higher than rPET/carb-MWNT composite 4 wt% (without CQD). It showed that the functionalization of CNTs with CQDs has following advantages: (i) it causes better dispersion of nanotubes in the rPET matrix which leads to their higher ability in sorption process, (ii) on the other hand, presence of CQDs provides extra functional groups and available sorption sites for pollutants elimination. It can be concluded that the greater MB sorption for the rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% was not only related to its higher SSA compared to the rPET, but also was due to the mesoroporous construction and plentiful surface functional groups attained by MWNT/CQDs. Digital images related to the rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%, and pure rPET before and after MB sorption were shown in Fig. 17d, as well.

As can be observed from Fig. 18 a, the removal efficiency of MB increased sharply as pH values were increased and it reached a plateau with the pH increasing to 8 and 10. The point of zero charge (PZC) is a factor which can explain the pH effect on MB sorption. It is the pH where the net entire charges of particles equals zero and is named pHPZC. It is the point where the pHfinal curve versus pHinitial crosses the pHfinal = pHinitial line. The relationship among the pHfinal and pHinitial values is also displayed in Fig. 18b. According to this Fig, pHPZC was 6.2 for rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%. Therefore, it can be concluded that the obtained composite is deprotonated and its surface obtains negative charges at pH above 6.2. More negative charges on the surface of rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% caused increasing of repulsion interactions between the adsorbents and providing more active sorption sites. On the other hand, negative surface of sorbent increased interactions with positively charged MB and improved sorption yield (Liu et al., 2015; Mallakpour and Behranvand, 2017e; Ezzeddine et al., 2016)

Fig. 18c displays the adsorbent dosage effect on the dye elimination efficiency with addition of different quantities of the rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% to 10 mL of MB solutions (10 mg L−1) and their shaking for 24 h at room temperature. The result showed that the removal efficiency of MB improved greatly from 69 to 90% when rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% dosage was increased from 20 to 60 mg which was owing to increase of available adsorption sites in rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%. The removal efficiency was reduced when dosage was reached to 70 mg. It may be outstanding to the sorption sites overlapping because of congestion of sorbent particles (Ossman and Mansour, 2013). Consequently, for the successive tests, dosage of 60 mg was chosen as the optimal sorbent dosages.

Fig. 18d shows the MB elimination over time by rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%. As can be observed MB was considerably adsorbed (about 80%) after 12 h and was almost completely adsorbed within two days. The initial adsorption step was quick which is corresponding to the MB adsorption on the external surface of the composite. The following step is a slow adsorption procedure. It is ascribed to the slow diffusion of MB molecules into the porous area of the adsorbent (such as MWNTs or CQDs) because at initial step, many of the accessible external sites have been occupied. The low rate of adsorption of composite may be owing to the long-range diffusion of MB molecules into the inner CNTs cavities or porous structures formed by aggregation (Li et al., 2013).

To examine the MB sorption kinetics, the obtained experimental data were fitted to pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order and intra particle diffusion models. The mentioned models are expressed as follow and the results are summarized in Table 3 :
(4)
Pseudo − first − order
ln
(
q e
−
q t
)
= ln
q e
−
k 1
t
(5)
Pseudo − second − order
t/
q t
= 1 /
k 2
q e 2
+t /
q e
(6)
Intra
particle
diffusion
q t
=
k
id
t
0.5
+C
where k1, k2 and kid (min−1, g mg−1 min−1, and mg g−1 min−0.5) are the rate constants of pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, and intra particle diffusion, respectively. qe and qt are the MB sorption capacity (mg g−1) at equilibrium and at a certain time t (min), respectively.

Fig. 19 shows all kinetics models. The pseudo-second-order model displayed a considerable regression (R2 = 0.9985). Moreover, the calculated equilibrium sorption capacity (1.64 mg g−1) from the pseudo-second-order model was fitted well with the experimental adsorption capacity (1.66 mg g−1) (Fig. 19). The pseudo-second-order model is based on the postulation that the chemical reaction is rate limiting step. This chemisorption can occur through electrons sharing or electrons exchange between the carboxyl groups present in the composite structure and MB.

To deeply examine the MB diffusion onto the obtained composite, the model of intra particle diffusion was employed to study the mechanism of diffusion throughout the sorption procedure. There were two linear parts and two stages during the sorption procedure. The first sharp slope explains the external diffusion with higher adsorption rate (K1d = 0.0804 mg g−1 min−0.5) compared to the second part (intra particle diffusion) (K2d = 0.0091 mg g−1 min−0.5). It is owing to the immediate accessibility of big active sorption positions on the rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% surface proposing that the external diffusion have principal role in the sorption kinetic.
The Langmuir, Freundlich, and Dubinin–Radushkevich (DR) isotherm models were used to study the kind of interaction between the MB and composite.

Related equations are shown below:
(7)
C e
/
q e
=
C e
/
q m
+ 1 /
K L
q m
Langmuir
(8)
ln
q e
= 1 / n
ln
C e
+ ln
K F
Freundlich
(9)
ln
q e
= l
nq m
−
K DR
ε 2
Dubinin − Radushkevich
where Ce (mg L−1) and qe (mg g−1) are the equilibrium MB concentrations and equilibrium sorption capacity, respectively. KL (L mg−1) is the Langmuir adsorption equilibrium constant and qm (mg g−1) denotes the maximum adsorption capacity. The KF and n parameters are the Freundlich equilibrium constants. In DR equation, KDR is the isotherm constant (mol2 kJ2), qm (mg g−1) represents the theoretical isotherm saturation capacity, and ε is Polanyi potential attained from:
(10)
ε=RT
ln
( 1 + 1 /
C e
)
where T (K) is the absolute temperature and R (8.314 J mol−1 K−1) is the gas constant, respectively.

All isotherm models accompanied by their equations are shown in Fig. 20 . The constants and correlation coefficient values extracted from Langmuir, Freundlich, and DR isotherms are listed in Table 4 . The experimental data match well more with Langmuir and DR models with higher correlation coefficients (0.9940 vs 0.9746). The Langmuir isotherm proposes a surface with homogeneous binding sites, equal adsorbate affinity, and no interaction between adsorbed species. It also shows that the MB adsorption by the rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% happens in a monolayer sorption manner.

The degree of appropriateness of sorbent to MB can be assessed by the amounts of the free energy, E (kJ mol−1) and the separation factor constant, RL, obtained from DR and Langmuir, respectively.
(11)
R L
= 1 /
1 +
K L
C 0
(12)
E= 1 / √ − 2 K
where C0 presents the initial MB concentration (mg L−1).

The RL values were calculated and all data were within the range of 0 < RL < 1, representing the sorption of MB on rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% was favorable. On the other hand, values of 0.1 < 1/n < 1 confirmed favorable adsorption of MB on the adsorbent.

The E value can provide significant information about mechanism of adsorption. E value lower than 8 kJ mol−1 shows physical sorption. While, 8 < E < 16 kJ mol−1 correspond to ion exchange (Ezzeddine et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2012; Mallakpour and Behranvand, 2017e). Corresponding to Table 4, the obtained E value was below 8 kJ mol−1 which proposes that the main interactions between the MB and rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% are physical adsorption such as H-bonding and electrostatic interaction between anionic carboxyl functional groups on the surfaces of CNTs and CQDs and cationic MB. Also, there are π-π attractions between aromatic skeleton of CNTs, and CQDs and aromatic blocks in the structures of PET by MB construction.

The maximum adsorption capacities (qmax) were compared to those reported in the literature (Table 5 ). Though, due to the dissimilar experimental conditions employed, a direct comparison of the prepared composite with other adsorbents is hard, it has been found that polymeric sorbents, like TiO2 doped sodium alginate crosslinked polyacrylic acid hydrogel (Thakur et al., 2016) and Gelatin-CNT-magnetic nanoparticles (Saber-samandari et al., 2017) showed qmax much higher than that of rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%, but they were more expensive sorbent too. Prepared sorbent in this study has various advantages: it was used from a trash for the manufacture of the rPET/MWNT/CQD composite. Consequently, in this way not only we deleted a trash from the environment but also used it for the water treatment. On the other hand, we combined cheap PET bottle with very small amount of functionalized CNTs. Surface functionalization of CNTs was done using green and low-cost source, a pepper. As it was shown in Fig. 17c, adsorption capacity was increased by adding low amount of functionalized CNTs. In addition, due to the existence of various and abundant functional groups in the construction of rPET/MWNT/CQD composite, it could be expected, this sorbent can adsorb other contaminants such as heavy metals and other dyes. Therefore, in view of both the practical and economical sides, rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% has great potential for use in pollutant removal from aqueous solution.

According to low surface area of the fabricated composite which was obtained through BET analysis, it may be concluded that different available surface functional groups present in the structure of CNTs, CQDs and PET play a key role in MB removal from aqueous solution. Cationic MB can be adsorbed easily by electrostatic forces on negatively charged surfaces of CNTs and CQDs (through interaction by anionic carboxyl functional groups in their structures). On the other hand, MB can be simply attached to the MWNTs, rPET, CQDs by π–π stacking interactions between the aromatic backbone of the dye, hexagonal skeleton of MWNTs, CQDs and aromatic part of rPET. However, according to the obtained kinetics results, electron exchange reaction can happen but is not a main mechanism in this study. Overall, the π–π stacking and electrostatic interactions between composite constituents and MB could be responsible for the sorption capability of the rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%, as schematically demonstrated in Fig. 21 .

To evaluate performance of the obtained composite for practical uses, reusability potential of sorbent is a significant factor. As can be observed in Fig. 22 , after four cycles, the removal efficiency of adsorbent reduced slightly and the dye elimination after one and four cycles was 98% and 92%. So, it could be concluded that regenerated composite were a good reusable sorbent and can be effectively employed for the dye recovery from water and wastewater.

Red pepper as a green source was used for the preparation of CQDs. The obtained CQDs were attached covalently on the surface of MWNTs with the help of CDI to obtain MWNT/CQDs hybrid. The CQDs existence on the surface of MWNTs was confirmed by FT-IR, XRD, TEM, AFM, N2 adsorption–desorption results, etc. Recyclable PET bottle and the obtained hybrid were employed to prepare mesoporous rPET/MWNT/CQD composite by a fast and simple method under sonication irradiations. The composite was used as a sorbent for the MB remediation from water solution. The adsorption outcomes showed that MB elimination process was dependent on initial pH, dosage, concentration and time. Favorable sorption was confirmed by RL and n parameters. The experimental results fitted well with Langmuir and DR isotherms than Freundlich isotherm which showed monolayer sorption. It could be concluded that physical adsorption such as H-bonding, electrostatic as well as π-π attractions, are the main interactions between the MB and rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt%. The kinetic adsorption followed the pseudo-second order model which approved chemisorption as well as physisorption mechanism. Also, rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% revealed high reusability and removal efficiencies. rPET/MWNT/CQD composite 4 wt% as a favorable sorbent for cationic dyes could be a hopeful candidate to remove other cationic pollutants such as heavy metals and other dyes from aqueous solutions. The present study is expected to open up novel opportunities in applying other recycled polymers and their reinforcement with various fillers for the effective pollutant removal from wastewater.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This current research was partially supported by Research Affairs Division Isfahan University of Technology (IUT), Isfahan, I. R. Iran. We are also thankful to Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council (INIC), Tehran, I. R. Iran, National Elite Foundation (NEF), Tehran, I. R. Iran, and Center of Excellence in Sensors and Green Chemistry (IUT), Isfahan, I. R. Iran.
